name: Social Content Calendar
description: Builds a 4-week multi-platform posting calendar from content pillars, cadence, and key dates. Use when planning a month of social content across channels.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Social Content Calendar
description: Builds a 4-week multi-platform posting calendar from content pillars, cadence, and key dates. Use when planning a month of social content across channels.
---
# Social Content Calendar

A calendar is not a spreadsheet of post ideas — it is a system that guarantees each channel gets the format and frequency it rewards, while keeping the brand's pillars balanced. Plan backward from outcomes and forward from the brand's real events.

## Start From Pillars and Ratios
Demand 3-5 content pillars before scheduling anything (e.g. education, behind-the-scenes, social proof, product, point-of-view). Apply a sane ratio: roughly 40% educational/value, 30% engagement/community, 20% promotional, 10% personality. If the user hands you only promo ideas, push back — feeds that are >25% selling decay reach fast.

## Match Cadence to Each Platform's Economy
Do not copy one frequency across channels. Sustainable defaults: LinkedIn 3-5x/week (weekday mornings), X/Twitter 1-3x/day (it rewards volume), Instagram 3-5x/week feed plus daily Stories, TikTok 1x/day minimum (the algorithm favors consistency over polish), YouTube Shorts 3-7x/week. Always cap at what the team can actually sustain — burning out at 2x is worse than 1x forever.

## Anchor to Real Dates, Not Filler
Layer in the brand's launches, events, and earnings before generic holidays. Then add only relevant cultural moments — skip the "National Donut Day" reflex unless it fits the brand. Leave 20% of slots open for reactive/trend posts; a fully booked calendar can't respond to what's happening.

## Build the Grid Week by Week
Output a table per week: date, platform, pillar, format (carousel/Reel/thread/text), working title, hook angle, CTA, asset needed, status. Sequence so a single idea feeds multiple slots across the month (plan the repurposing, don't improvise it). Front-load production-heavy formats early in the week.

## Flag Capacity and Dependencies
Call out where the plan needs design, video, or approvals, and which posts block others. Note batching opportunities — same shoot, multiple posts.

## When to Diverge
If the account is under ~2k followers, prioritize one channel done well over four done thinly, and tell the user so. If they have no analytics yet, schedule a 4-week test mix and mark it as a baseline to measure, not a finished system.

name: Social Caption Writer
description: Writes platform-native captions for Instagram, LinkedIn, X, and TikTok from a topic and brand voice with correct length and CTA. Use when drafting the words that ship with a post.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Social Caption Writer
description: Writes platform-native captions for Instagram, LinkedIn, X, and TikTok from a topic and brand voice with correct length and CTA. Use when drafting the words that ship with a post.
---
# Social Caption Writer

A caption earns the first line or it earns nothing — the rest is only read by people the hook already won. Write for the specific platform's reading posture, not a generic "social" blob, and let brand voice live in every sentence rather than a tacked-on sign-off.

## Front-Load the Hook, Always
Every platform truncates. Put the most arresting idea in the first sentence — before the "...more" cutoff (~125 chars on Instagram, ~140 on LinkedIn, one breath on TikTok). Never open with "In today's fast-paced world" or throat-clearing context. Lead with tension, a number, a contrarian claim, or a stakes-raising question.

## Hit the Native Length and Shape
Lengths that perform: Instagram 138-150 chars for punchy or 1-3 tight paragraphs for storytelling; LinkedIn 1300-2000 chars with one-sentence lines and white space between them (no walls of text); X/Twitter under 280, ideally ~100 so it breathes; TikTok caption short and curiosity-driven since the video carries the load. Use line breaks generously on IG and LinkedIn — mobile reading needs air.

## One CTA, Earned
Give exactly one ask per caption and make it specific: "Save this for your next launch" beats "engage with us." Match the CTA to platform behavior — comments and saves on Instagram, thoughtful replies on LinkedIn, reply/repost on X, follow/duet on TikTok. Skip "link in bio" desperation; reference it only when there is a real reason to leave the app.

## Keep Brand Voice in the Verbs
Voice is carried by word choice and rhythm, not adjectives. If the brand is dry and technical, cut the exclamation points. If it's playful, let sentence fragments and asides through. Mirror back the voice traits the user gave you; if they gave none, ask for 2-3 adjectives and one phrase they'd never say.

## Avoid Penalized Patterns
No engagement-bait ("comment YES below," tag-3-friends chains, follow-for-follow) — platforms suppress it. No keyword stuffing in the caption body. Emojis as accents, not punctuation replacements.

## When to Hand Back
If the topic is thin or off-brand, say so and propose a sharper angle rather than dressing up nothing. If the asset (image/video) isn't described, ask — the caption's job depends on what it's paired with.

name: Social Hook Generator
description: Produces 10 scroll-stopping opening lines for a given post idea, organized by hook archetype. Use when a post needs a stronger first line or you want options to test.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Social Hook Generator
description: Produces 10 scroll-stopping opening lines for a given post idea, organized by hook archetype. Use when a post needs a stronger first line or you want options to test.
---
# Social Hook Generator

The hook does one job: buy the second line. On every feed the first 1-2 seconds (or the first ~70 visible characters) decide whether the rest exists. Generate variety on purpose — different archetypes win on different platforms and audiences, so the user can test rather than guess.

## Work Across Hook Archetypes
Produce 10 hooks spanning distinct patterns so they aren't ten flavors of the same line: the Contrarian ("Stop doing X"), the Stakes ("This mistake cost me $40k"), the Curiosity Gap ("Nobody tells you what happens after Y"), the Specific Number ("I tested 7 ways to Z"), the Question that names the reader's pain, the Mini-Story open ("3 years ago I almost quit"), the Bold Claim, the How-To promise, the Callout ("If you're a [role], read this"), and the Pattern Interrupt (unexpected first word). Label each hook with its archetype.

## Make Them Concrete
Vague hooks die. Push for specifics from the post idea — real numbers, named outcomes, a timeframe, a dollar figure. "I grew fast" is dead; "I went from 0 to 10k in 90 days" stops the scroll. If the source idea lacks specifics, infer plausible placeholders and mark them so the user fills in true ones.

## Respect Platform Reading Posture
For TikTok/Reels, write hooks that work as the first spoken line AND on-screen text — short, punchy, often a question or claim. For LinkedIn, the hook is text-first and can carry a touch more nuance, but must land before the "...more" fold. For X, the whole opening tweet is the hook; make it self-contained. For Instagram, the first line above the fold does the work.

## Earn the Curiosity Honestly
The hook must be a true promise the post pays off — open loops the body closes. Clickbait that the content doesn't deliver trains the algorithm and the audience against you. No "you won't BELIEVE" hollow shock.

## Avoid Penalized and Stale Patterns
No engagement-bait phrasing, no "Let that sink in," no "Unpopular opinion" unless the take is genuinely unpopular. Vary sentence length and avoid starting every hook with "I."

## When to Push Back
If the post idea has no real tension or payoff, say the problem is upstream and help reframe the idea before polishing hooks for it.

name: Cross-Platform Reformatter
description: Reshapes one piece of content into platform-specific versions tuned for each channel's length, tone, format, and native conventions. Use when adapting a post, article, or video across multiple platforms.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Cross-Platform Reformatter
description: Reshapes one piece of content into platform-specific versions tuned for each channel's length, tone, format, and native conventions. Use when adapting a post, article, or video across multiple platforms.
---
# Cross-Platform Reformatter

Reposting the same text everywhere is the fastest way to look like a bot and underperform on every channel. Reformatting means re-expressing the core idea in each platform's native grammar — its length, tone, structure, and what its algorithm rewards — not copy-pasting with hashtags swapped.

## Extract the Core, Then Rebuild
First pull out the single transferable idea and its proof (the stat, story, or takeaway). That core stays constant; everything else is rebuilt per platform. Never just truncate the long version to fit — a shorter post needs a different structure, not fewer words.

## Translate Into Each Platform's Grammar
LinkedIn: text-first, 1300-2000 chars, short lines with white space, professional-but-human, one clear insight. X/Twitter: either a tight single post under 280 or a thread where each tweet stands alone; punchy, lower-case-friendly, no corporate tone. Instagram: visual-led caption 138-150 chars or a short narrative; the image/Reel carries weight. TikTok: a spoken script with a verbal hook in the first 2 seconds; conversational, not written-to-be-read. YouTube Shorts/Reels: same script logic with on-screen text beats. Newsletter: longest form, can keep nuance the social cuts drop.

## Respect Native Conventions
Match each channel's real norms: hashtags 3-5 on Instagram and 1-2 on LinkedIn/X, none forced on TikTok beyond a couple discovery tags. Use platform features as intended — carousels and Reels on IG, document/carousel posts and polls on LinkedIn, threads and quote-posts on X. Adjust emoji density to voice and platform.

## Preserve Voice, Shift Register
The brand stays recognizable, but register flexes: more formal on LinkedIn, looser on TikTok and X. Keep signature phrasing; drop jargon that doesn't travel to a casual feed.

## Sequence and Stagger
When output feeds a calendar, note ordering — long-form first (blog/newsletter), then atomized social, so each piece points back to the anchor. Suggest staggering by days, not dumping all versions at once.

## When Not to Reformat
If a piece genuinely fits only one channel (a niche technical thread, a platform-specific trend), say so rather than forcing weak versions onto channels where it will flop.

name: Hashtag Strategist
description: Recommends a tiered hashtag and keyword set sized to account reach, balancing broad, niche, and branded tags while avoiding banned or spammy ones. Use when choosing tags for a post or building a reusable set.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Hashtag Strategist
description: Recommends a tiered hashtag and keyword set sized to account reach, balancing broad, niche, and branded tags while avoiding banned or spammy ones. Use when choosing tags for a post or building a reusable set.
---
# Hashtag Strategist

Hashtags are a discovery and categorization tool, not a magic reach lever — and on several platforms keyword-rich captions now matter more than tags. The goal is to put a post in front of a reachable, relevant audience, which means matching tag competitiveness to the account's actual size.

## Tier by Reach, Don't Chase Volume
Build three tiers. Broad/high-volume (1M+ posts): one or two only — a small account will be buried in seconds, so these are aspirational reach, not the workhorse. Niche/mid (10k-500k posts): the core of the set, specific enough that the post can rank and stay visible. Branded/community (your own + small community tags): always include the brand tag and 1-2 community ones to build a searchable archive. The smaller the account, the more the set should skew niche and small.

## Size the Set to the Platform
Instagram: 3-8 well-chosen tags outperform the 30-tag dump — relevance beats quantity and the maximum is a ceiling, not a target. TikTok: 3-5 tags mixing one broad discovery tag with specific topical ones; the caption keywords and on-screen text matter as much. LinkedIn: 1-3 only, and they should read naturally. X/Twitter: 1-2 max — more looks spammy and hurts. YouTube: tags plus, more importantly, keyword-rich titles and descriptions.

## Treat Keywords as First-Class
Instagram, TikTok, and LinkedIn now surface content via caption and on-screen text in search. Recommend the literal search phrases a target viewer would type, woven into the caption naturally — not only hashtags. This is often higher-leverage than the tag list itself.

## Vet Every Tag
Screen for banned, broken, or shadow-flagged tags (these silently suppress a post) and for tags hijacked by unrelated or spammy content. Avoid tags so generic they're pure noise. Reject follow-for-follow and engagement-pod tags outright — they attract bots and can flag the account.

## Build Rotating Sets, Not One Block
Give 2-3 interchangeable sets so the account isn't posting an identical tag block every time (which can look automated). Tie each set to a content pillar.

## When Tags Won't Help
If the account's problem is content quality or consistency, say so — no hashtag set rescues a post nobody wants to watch. For very small or hyper-niche brands, recommend leaning on keywords and community tags over reach tags entirely.

name: Engagement Reply Drafter
description: Drafts on-brand replies to comments and DMs at scale, including graceful handling of criticism and complaints. Use when responding to an inbox or comment section in the brand's voice.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Engagement Reply Drafter
description: Drafts on-brand replies to comments and DMs at scale, including graceful handling of criticism and complaints. Use when responding to an inbox or comment section in the brand's voice.
---
# Engagement Reply Drafter

Replies are the most underrated growth lever on social — early comment engagement signals the algorithm and turns followers into a community. But canned, identical replies read as bot behavior and erode trust. Every reply should sound like a specific human on the brand team responding to a specific person.

## Triage Before Drafting
Sort each item into a lane and reply accordingly: genuine praise (acknowledge specifically, don't just "thanks!"), a question (answer it directly, then optionally invite more), a complaint or bug (empathize, own it, move to DM/support), criticism or trolling (assess fair vs. bad-faith), spam/abuse (don't engage — hide or block). Mismatched lane equals wrong tone.

## Make Replies Specific and Short
Reference what the person actually said — name their detail, not a generic platitude. Keep most replies to 1-2 sentences; social comments aren't email. Ask a light return question on positive comments to extend the thread (more comments = more reach), but only when it's natural, never forced.

## Handle Criticism Gracefully
For fair criticism: acknowledge it sincerely, take responsibility without defensiveness, state what you'll do, and resist the urge to over-explain. Never argue publicly or get sarcastic — screenshots are forever. For complaints with private details (orders, accounts), reply publicly with brief empathy then take it to DMs. For bad-faith trolling, the brand-safe move is usually a brief, unbothered reply or no reply at all — do not feed it. Recommend deletion only for harassment or slurs, not for negative-but-honest feedback.

## Keep Voice Consistent, Replies Varied
The brand voice stays constant; the wording must vary across replies so a comment section doesn't show the same phrase ten times. If given voice traits, mirror them; default to warm, concise, and human. Match the commenter's energy within brand bounds — playful to playful, calm to upset.

## Know the Escalation Line
Flag anything legal, safety-related, a refund/financial dispute, a press or influencer account, or a viral pile-on for a human decision-maker — draft a holding reply but mark it for review, don't fire it blind.

## When Not to Reply
Some comments are best left alone: pure trolling, obvious bait, and arguments you can't win. Saying "no reply" is a valid, often correct recommendation — note it explicitly.

name: Carousel Scripter
description: Outlines a multi-slide Instagram or LinkedIn carousel with a hook slide, per-slide copy, and a CTA slide. Use when turning an idea into a swipeable, value-dense carousel.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Carousel Scripter
description: Outlines a multi-slide Instagram or LinkedIn carousel with a hook slide, per-slide copy, and a CTA slide. Use when turning an idea into a swipeable, value-dense carousel.
---
# Carousel Scripter

A carousel works because every swipe is a micro-commitment, and swipe-throughs are a strong signal both Instagram and LinkedIn reward. The whole format lives or dies on slide one earning the swipe, and each slide after it earning the next. Design for momentum, one idea per slide, never a paragraph dumped onto a frame.

## Slide One Is the Whole Game
The cover must stop the scroll and promise a payoff: a bold claim, a number ("7 mistakes..."), a contrarian take, or a clear transformation. Keep it to a short headline plus a visual cue to swipe. If slide one is weak, nothing else matters — spend disproportionate effort here and offer 2-3 cover options.

## Structure for Momentum
Use a proven spine: Hook (slide 1) then Stakes/why-it-matters (slide 2) then Value slides (3 through n-1, one point each) then CTA (final slide). Aim for 6-10 slides; fewer than 5 feels thin, more than 12 loses people. End each value slide with a tiny open loop or forward cue so the reader wants the next one. Number slides so progress is visible.

## One Idea, Few Words Per Slide
Each slide carries a single point: a punchy slide headline (the part skimmers read) plus a line or two of support. Roughly 1-2 short sentences per slide max — these are read on a phone at a glance. Front-load the slide's point in its first words. Write the headline layer so the carousel makes sense even if someone only reads headlines.

## End With One Clear Ask
The final slide converts attention into action: one CTA only — follow for more, save this, comment a keyword, or visit a specific resource. Tie it to the value just delivered ("Save this before your next launch"). Don't stack three asks. Pair the carousel with a caption that restates the hook and adds context the slides couldn't hold.

## Platform Nuances
LinkedIn carousels (PDF/document posts) skew educational, frameworks, and professional value — more text tolerated, cleaner design. Instagram skews visual, punchier text, stronger design dependency. Note design direction per slide (what the visual should show) since copy and layout are inseparable here.

## When a Carousel Is the Wrong Format
If the idea is a single quick tip or a time-sensitive moment, a Reel, single image, or text post will outperform a carousel — say so. Carousels earn their keep on multi-step, list, or framework content, not one-liners.
